:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 6,241 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 29% of the prison population.



``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

Each felony in Oklahoma has a statutory punishment; there are currently no “classes” or “levels” of crimes as found in other states. Inmates convicted of crimes committed on or after July 1, 1998 are eligible for parole release after serving one-third of their sentence, unless they are serving a life sentence without the possibility of parole. Inmates serving consecutive sentences are not eligible for parole consideration until one-third of the consecutive sentence has been served. However, Oklahoma has enacted truth in sentencing laws which require inmates convicted of certain crimes to serve at least 85% of their sentence of imprisonment without any reduction due to sentencing credits. Some crimes, such as first-degree rape, have statutory minimum penalties. There are also statutory minimum penalties for second or subsequent convictions for certain crimes.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison for a new crime, accounting for 46% (1,140 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ### Age ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 2,499 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 40% of the parole-eligible prison population in prison for new crimes.

``` ::: :::

## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Descriptive Statistics

``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Race, ethincity, age, and gender

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Relative Rate Index

The relative rate index (RRI) provides a measure of disparity in outcomes for different racial and ethnic groups by comparing the rates at which individuals of different races and ethnic groups experience incarceration. The rate for each specific racial and ethnic group is computed by dividing the population of interest within that group by the total state population for that particular group.

To calculate the RRI, the rate of each outcome of interest for individuals who are Black non-Hispanic and Hispanic any race, is divided by the rate at which the outcome occurs for individuals who are White. A RRI of 1 indicates no disparity. RRIs greater than 1 indicate the outcome occurs more frequently for individuals who are Black or Hispanic, and an RRI less than 1 indicates an outcome occurs less frequently than for individuals who are White.
